1: μετὰ ταῦτα (meta tauta) is the Evangelist's typical narrative connector — "after these things" — marking a new episode without specifying the exact interval. περιεπάτει (imperfect of G4043 peripateō) is continuous: Jesus was going about in Galilee over a period. The imperfect of ζητεῖν + ἀποκτεῖναι ("seeking to kill") mirrors it: the Judean leaders' lethal intent is not a single decision but an ongoing disposition, set up in the aftermath of John 5.
2: σκηνοπηγία (G4634, from skēnē, tent, + pēgnymi, to fasten/pitch) is the Greek term for the Feast of Tabernacles — literally "tent-pitching." It was the seven-day harvest festival of Tishri (September–October), during which pilgrims constructed and lived in temporary shelters. The Evangelist identifies it as "the feast of the Jews" (hē heortē tōn Ioudaiōn) — his standard distancing formula — without yet indicating which day matters most (that comes at v.37).
3: ἀδελφοί (G80, brothers) — already established as separate from the circle of disciples (John 2:12); the Evangelist will confirm their unbelief at v.5. Their imperative μετάβηθι (metabēthi, "depart/move away") implies they regarded Galilee as too marginal for the kind of visibility Jesus needed. ἔργα (G2041, works) picks up the language of chapters 5–6; they want the works performed publicly so the μαθηταί (disciples, G3101) in Judea can see them — assuming Jesus's goal is maximum audience, which his response refutes.
4: ἐν κρυπτῷ (G2927, in secret) vs. ἐν παρρησίᾳ (G3954, openly/publicly) — the brothers frame the choice as a PR logic: no one who seeks public recognition acts in secret. Their irony is that Jesus has been deliberately avoiding the kind of self-display they are urging. The verb φανέρωσον (G5319 aorist imperative, "show/manifest yourself") recurs in John's theology as a divine disclosure term (cf. 1:31; 2:11); here the brothers use it in a worldly register.
5: οὐδὲ γὰρ οἱ ἀδελφοὶ αὐτοῦ ἐπίστευον εἰς αὐτόν — the Evangelist's parenthetical aside. The imperfect ἐπίστευον (G4100) marks persistent, ongoing unbelief, not a single refusal. Eis auton (into him) is the Johannine construction for full, trusting belief — what the brothers lack is not merely intellectual assent but this orientation of the whole person toward Jesus.
6: ὁ καιρὸς ὁ ἐμός (ho kairos ho emos) — the Evangelist uses G2540 kairos (appointed season, right occasion) here and in v.8, in contrast with the brothers' time which is "always ready." This is different from G5610 hōra ("his hour") which appears at v.30 — kairos indicates the divinely ordered moment for a particular act; hōra carries the weight of the hour of death-and-glorification. πάντοτε ἕτοιμός ἐστιν (always ready, present tense) signals that the world operates without this constraint: for those aligned with it, any occasion will do.
7: ὁ κόσμος ὑμᾶς οὐ δύναται μισεῖν — the world cannot hate the brothers because they belong to it; hatred of those who are its own would be self-contradiction. ἐμὲ δὲ μισεῖ (but me it hates) — the adversative is sharp. The reason: ὅτι ἐγὼ μαρτυρῶ (because I testify, G3140 present active), his ongoing witness that the world's ἔργα (works) are πονηρά (G4190, evil). Martyreō is a key Johannine verb; here it explains not devotion but hostility — truthful witness provokes hatred.
8: ὁ καιρὸς ὁ ἐμὸς οὔπω πεπλήρωται — the perfect tense of πληρόω (G4137, to fill/fulfill) is significant: "my time has not yet been brought to its fullness." The use of the perfect (result-state) rather than a simple present intensifies the idea of completeness: the kairos will only be real when it has arrived in its full form. This is not hesitation but theological precision about timing. Some MSS read οὔπω (not yet) vs. οὐκ (not), a variant noted in Ellicott; the weight of manuscript evidence favors οὔπω.
9: A transitional verse: Jesus remains in Galilee after sending his brothers ahead. The imperfect ἔμεινεν (G3306, remained) reinforces that this was not a momentary pause. The brevity matches the verse's function — a narrative spacer that makes the secret journey of v.10 deliberate, not coincidental.
10: ἐν κρυπτῷ (G2927, in secret/privately) vs. φανερῶς (G5320, openly) — two adverbs that bracket the paradox of v.10: he went, but not publicly. The construction ὡς ἐν κρυπτῷ ("as it were secretly") marks the manner of travel, not concealment of identity. Jesus arrives at the feast that he told his brothers was not his time — but the Evangelist has already established why: it was not the right kairos for the public self-presentation the brothers wanted; arriving quietly is different from not arriving.
11: οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι (hoi Ioudaioi) — the Evangelist's term here (as in vv.1,13,15) for the Jerusalem religious establishment, not the crowds. Their imperfect ἐζήτουν (G2212, were seeking) continues the hostile searching of v.1. The phrase Ποῦ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος; ("Where is that man?") — ekeinos carries a distancing force, almost contemptuous: "that person."
12: γογγυσμὸς (G1112, murmuring/grumbling) echoes the vocabulary used in the LXX for Israel's wilderness murmuring against Moses and God (Exod 16:7–12; Num 14:27). The Evangelist deploys it to mark divided, suppressed opinion in the crowds. The two camps: ἀγαθός (G18, good/morally upright) vs. πλανᾷ τὸν ὄχλον (he deceives the crowd, G4105 present indicative: is leading astray). ὄχλος (G3793) here appears twice — the MKT renders "the people"; the word refers to the common crowd of festival pilgrims, distinct from hoi Ioudaioi.
13: παρρησίᾳ (G3954, bold/open speech) — no one was speaking publicly about him because of τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων (the fear of the Judean authorities). The word parrēsia carries a political weight: it is the speech of the free person who stands in the assembly without hedging. Its suppression here is a symptom of the social coercion exercised by the Jerusalem establishment.
14: ἱερόν (G2411, the temple complex — courts and precincts, not the naos/inner sanctuary) — same distinction noted at 2:14 vs. 2:19 in chapters 1–2. The Evangelist notes Jesus arrived εἰς μέσον τῆς ἑορτῆς (in the middle of the feast, the Chōl Moēd), not on the opening days. ἐδίδασκεν (imperfect, G1321: was teaching) marks sustained activity.
15: Πῶς οὗτος γράμματα οἶδεν μὴ μεμαθηκώς; — γράμματα (G1121, literally "letters") refers to scribal learning, the rabbinic education acquired under an accredited teacher. The Judean leaders' amazement is at the incongruity: he has the rhetorical competence of a trained rabbi without the formal credential. This is distinct from ignorance; they are not surprised he can read, but that he commands the interpretive tradition without institutional authorization.
16: ἡ ἐμὴ διδαχὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμή — a grammatical paradox: "my teaching is not mine." Both uses of emē (G1699, mine) are identical in form but different in force: the first identifies it as his, the second denies ownership. The resolution: it is genuinely his — he teaches it — but its source is the one who sent him, so it does not originate from him. This mirrors the Johannine pattern throughout: the Son does nothing of himself (5:19); he speaks the Father's words. ἀλλὰ τοῦ πέμψαντός με (but of the one who sent me) — the participial form "the one who sent me" is a near-formula for the Father across the discourse.
17: ἐὰν τις θέλῃ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ποιεῖν — the condition for knowing whether Jesus's teaching is from God is moral, not epistemic: "if anyone wills to do his will." The verb θέλῃ (G2309, subjunctive: wills/is willing) is not mere intention but active orientation; it is the posture of the will that precedes knowledge. γνώσεται (G1097, future: will come to know) — knowing is the outcome of this alignment, not its prerequisite. The Evangelist here encapsulates a fundamental Johannine epistemology: moral will governs theological perception.
18: ὁ ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ λαλῶν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἰδίαν ζητεῖ — the one who speaks from himself seeks his own δόξαν (G1391, glory/reputation). The test of authentic messenger-ship is the object of the seeking: self-glorification (G2398 idian, his own) vs. the glory of the one who sent him. ἀδικία (G93, unrighteousness/injustice) is absent from the one who seeks the Sender's glory — the claim shifts the conversation from biographical to ethical: his motives are verifiable.
19: Οὐ Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὸν νόμον; (Has not Moses given you the law?) — the perfect δέδωκεν (G1325, has given, result-state: it stands given to you) contrasts with what follows: and yet none of you keeps it. νόμον (G3551, dl=3) — "law" throughout the chapter; the dispute level reflects the law's range across Torah, Mosaic code, and broader principle. The rhetorical charge is precise: they appeal to Moses while seeking to kill the one Moses pointed toward (5:46).
20: δαιμόνιον ἔχεις (G1140, you have a demon) — the crowd's retort is a standard deflection: those attributed with unusual claims were routinely accused of demonic possession (cf. Matt 11:18; John 8:48). The ὄχλος here (the festival crowd, G3793) is distinct from the Jerusalem residents of v.25 who know about the murder plot; these pilgrims are genuinely unaware and so genuinely incredulous at the claim.
21: ἓν ἔργον ἐποίησα (one work I did) — the singular ērgon refers to the healing of John 5:1–16 on the Sabbath in Jerusalem. πάντες θαυμάζετε (G2296, you all marvel) — thaumazete is presented as an ongoing state: they continue to be astonished over it. The verse is brief but pivots the discourse to the circumcision-sabbath argument that follows.
22: Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὴν περιτομήν — περιτομή (G4061, circumcision) has priority over Sabbath by Torah logic: if the eighth day of a male infant's life falls on a Sabbath, circumcision proceeds. Jesus's argument here cites the law's own internal hierarchy. οὐχ ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ Μωϋσέως ἐστίν, ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ τῶν πατέρων (not from Moses but from the fathers) — circumcision predates Sinai (Gen 17); Moses encoded it but did not originate it.
23: ὅλον ἄνθρωπον ὑγιῆ ἐποίησα — G3650 holon (the whole, every part) + G5199 hygiē (whole/sound) together: "I made a whole person completely well." The logic inverts the circumcision precedent: if partial physical modification (circumcision of one member) overrides Sabbath to fulfill Torah, how much more does making the entire person whole? χωλεύετε (lit. "do you limp/are you angry", G5520 — the verb used here is for being angry) — the irritation is presented as inconsistency, not moral failure per se.
24: μὴ κρίνετε κατ᾽ ὄψιν — κρίνετε (G2919, present imperative: stop judging) κατ᾽ ὄψιν (G3799, according to appearance/face). Surface compliance with Sabbath versus the healing of a whole person: the appearance-based ruling condemns what the deeper logic of the law permits. δικαίαν κρίσιν κρίνατε (G1342 + G2920: judge a righteous judgment) — the aorist imperative here calls for a single definitive act of right assessment; dikaios (G1342 dl=4) in this range means "equitable / consistent with the norm of the law."
25: τινὲς ἐκ τῶν Ἱεροσολυμιτῶν (some of the Jerusalem residents) — distinct from the Galilean pilgrims of v.20; these locals know the authorities' intent. Their question Οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν ὃν ζητοῦσιν ἀποκτεῖναι; (Is this not the one they are seeking to kill?) opens a new layer of irony: he speaks publicly and they do nothing. The imperfect ζητοῦσιν (were seeking) is continuous.
26: παρρησίᾳ λαλεῖ (he speaks publicly/boldly, G3954) — the same word suppressed in v.13 now describes Jesus's open teaching in the temple. The Jerusalemites' inference — μήποτε ἀληθῶς ἔγνωσαν οἱ ἄρχοντες (could the rulers have truly come to know?) — captures the gap between what the authorities know and how they act. ἄρχοντες (G758, rulers = the Sanhedrin elite) are the ones whose silence is politically significant.
27: ἀλλὰ τοῦτον οἴδαμεν πόθεν ἐστίν (but this man — we know where he is from) — the Jerusalemites immediately correct their own question. The messianic expectation they invoke — ὁ δὲ χριστὸς ὅταν ἔρχηται, οὐδεὶς γινώσκει πόθεν ἐστίν (when the Christ comes, no one knows where he is from) — reflects a strand of Second Temple expectation that the Messiah would appear suddenly and obscurely, not from a known family and village. John does not confirm or deny the tradition as such; the irony operates on another level — they think they know where Jesus is from and are wrong about what that knowledge means.
28: ἔκραξεν (G2896, aorist: cried out) — the verb marks a sudden, forceful elevation of voice; the Evangelist uses it for moments of urgent Christological declaration (cf. 1:15; 7:37; 12:44). κἀμὲ οἴδατε καὶ οἴδατε πόθεν εἰμί (both me you know, and where I am from you know) — the concessive irony: Jesus grants their claim of knowing. What follows ("I have not come from myself…") undercuts what their knowing amounts to: they know the Galilean; they do not know the one who sent him, who is ἀληθινός (G228, true/genuine, not merely truthful but authentically real).
29: κἀγὼ οἶδα αὐτόν (and I know him) — emphatic egō placed first; the contrast is absolute. His knowing is based on: ὅτι παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἰμι (because I am from him, G3844 denoting origin: from beside him). The preposition para (from beside/from the side of) carries relational weight — not merely factual origin but essential proximity. κἀκεῖνός με ἀπέστειλεν (and that one sent me) — the perfect form of the relationship with the Father grounds the sending.
30: ἐζήτουν οὖν αὐτὸν πιάσαι (they were seeking therefore to seize him) — imperfect of repeated failed attempts. The reason for restraint: ὅτι οὔπω ἐληλύθει ἡ ὥρα αὐτοῦ (because his hōra had not yet come). Here G5610 hōra replaces the kairos of vv.6,8 — hōra is the appointed hour of death-and-glorification, the eschatological moment toward which the entire Gospel is oriented (cf. 12:23; 13:1; 17:1). The crowd's inability to seize Jesus is not tactical but theological: the hour governs the narrative.
31: ἐκ τοῦ ὄχλου δὲ πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν (many of the crowd believed in him) — the Johannine pisteuein eis construction; this is crowd-faith in its provisional form. Their criterion: σημεῖα (G4592, signs) — would the Messiah do more sēmeia than this man? The Evangelist consistently uses sēmeia, not dynameis (mighty works); signs point beyond themselves to the one performing them.
32: ἤκουσαν οἱ Φαρισαῖοι τοῦ ὄχλου γογγύζοντος (the Pharisees heard the crowd murmuring) — G1111 gongyzontos (present participle: as they were murmuring), a slightly different form from the G1112 noun of v.12 but same root. The institutional response: ἀπέστειλαν ὑπηρέτας (sent officers/temple servants, G5257) to arrest him — the machinery of official suppression is set in motion, ironically during a feast celebrating God's provision.
33: ἔτι χρόνον μικρὸν μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι (still a little chronos I am with you) — G5550 chronos here, not kairos or hōra: bare duration, the neutral "time." Jesus frames the officers' mission as already irrelevant; his departure is on a different timetable than their arrest plan. πρὸς τὸν πέμψαντά με (toward the one who sent me) — going to the Father is the goal of the departure, not escape from arrest.
34: ζητήσετέ με καὶ οὐχ εὑρήσετε (you will seek me and not find) — two futures: zētēsete (G2212) and heurēsete (G2147). The seeking after he is gone will be fruitless — not because he hides but because the destination (ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγώ, where I am) is inaccessible to those who have refused the path that leads there. This is not vindictiveness but consequence: the seeking without the will to do God's will (v.17) produces nothing.
35: ἡ διασπορά (G1290, the Diaspora — the technical term for Jews scattered among Gentile nations) — the Judean leaders' confusion generates an ironic prophecy: they joke that perhaps he will go and teach Ἕλληνας (G1672, Greeks/Gentiles). The Evangelist records this as dramatic irony; the mission to the Gentiles does follow from Jesus's work, though not in the way they intend. Diaspora is a Jewish term; its use here signals they are not imagining a Jewish audience among the nations but a Gentile one.
36: Τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ λόγος (What is this logos?) — G3056 logos here functions as "saying/utterance," the common Greek sense, not the cosmic Word of 1:1. The authorities' scorn does not answer the question; it registers puzzlement (θαυμάζω implied in the repetition of the saying). Their inability to parse the departure-language reflects the epistemological failure named at v.17: they have not willed to do the Father's will.
37: ἐν δὲ τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ μεγάλῃ τῆς ἑορτῆς (on the last great day of the feast) — the Evangelist marks this moment with unusual temporal weight; eschata hēmera and megalē together are superlatives. Whether the seventh or eighth day is debated; the Mishnah describes a water-libation ceremony during Tabernacles (Sukkah 4:9–10) in which water from the Pool of Siloam was poured out — providing immediate context for the living water imagery. ἔκραξεν (cried out, G2896 aorist) — the voice elevated for the crowd; φωνῇ μεγάλῃ (with a great/loud voice) amplifies the urgency of the invitation.
38: ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμέ, καθὼς εἶπεν ἡ γραφή (the one who believes in me, as the Scripture said) — the citation formula kathōs eipen hē graphē (as the Scripture said) is used for a text the audience should recognize, but no single OT passage says rivers of living water will flow from within him (or "from his belly," G2836 koilia). The likely referents are Zech 14:8 (living waters flowing from Jerusalem), Ezek 47:1–12 (the river from the temple), and Isa 58:11 (like a spring whose waters do not fail). The citation is thematic rather than verbatim; John treats the composite of water-from-the-holy-place imagery as a single scriptural witness.
39: τοῦτο δὲ εἶπεν περὶ τοῦ πνεύματος (G4151, dl=3) — the Evangelist's explanatory aside is rare and important: he tells us what the saying means. Pneuma can mean wind, breath, or spirit; here it is definitively the Spirit given to believers. The explanatory clause οὔπω γὰρ ἦν πνεῦμα, ὅτι Ἰησοῦς οὐδέπω ἐδοξάσθη (not yet was [the] Spirit, because Jesus had not yet been glorified) does not assert the Spirit did not exist, but that this specific gift — the Spirit given to those who believe — awaits the glorification of Jesus. δοξάζω (G1392, glorify) — the Johannine term for death-and-exaltation; the Spirit's coming is indexed to that event (cf. 16:7).
40: ὁ προφήτης (the Prophet, with definite article) — distinct from "a prophet"; this refers to the prophet-like-Moses promised in Deut 18:15–18 ("a prophet from among you, from your brothers, like me"). The crowd identifies Jesus with this figure, not yet with the Messiah — in the Second Temple period these were sometimes distinguished (cf. John 1:20–21 where the Baptist is asked separately about "the Prophet" and "the Christ").
41: Ἄλλοι ἔλεγον Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ χριστός (others said, This one is the Christ) — a step beyond "the Prophet." The objection immediately raised: μὴ γὰρ ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας ὁ χριστὸς ἔρχεται; (the Messiah does not come from Galilee, does he?) — the Greek particle mē expects a negative answer. The messianic expectation grounded in Mic 5:2 and 2 Sam 7 requires a Davidic-Bethlehemite origin, which Galilean provenance seems to contradict. The Evangelist records this without correction — the irony is that the objectors are theologically right (the Messiah does come from Bethlehem) but factually uninformed about Jesus's actual birth.
42: ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος Δαυίδ (from the seed of David) and ἀπὸ Βηθλέεμ (from Bethlehem) — the crowd cites Mic 5:2 without naming it. γραφή (G1124, scripture) invoked as the authority. The poignant detail: in citing scripture correctly, they disqualify themselves from recognizing its fulfillment because they do not know Jesus was born in Bethlehem. The Evangelist never narrates the birth of Jesus; he lets this misapprehension stand as a sign of broader spiritual misreading.
43: σχίσμα οὖν ἐγένετο (G4978, a schism therefore arose) — schisma (from schizō, to split/tear) is used here, in 9:16, and in 10:19 of divisions caused by Jesus. The word implies a structural split in a previously unified group, not just disagreement. It is the Evangelist's marker that Jesus's presence forces a decision that cannot be deferred.
44: Verse 44 repeats the motif of v.30: οὐδεὶς ἐπέβαλεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸν τὰς χεῖρας (no one laid hands on him). Here v.30's reason (his hour had not come) is not restated — the Evangelist lets the structural parallel speak. The τινὲς ἐξ αὐτῶν (some of them) who wanted to seize him are presumably those who denied his messianic claim on geographical grounds (v.41).
45: ὑπηρέται (G5257, officers/temple servants) return to the chief priests and Pharisees. The question Διὰ τί οὐκ ἠγάγετε αὐτόν; (Why did you not bring him?) reveals the Sanhedrin expected a successful arrest. The Greek construction places the question's weight on dia ti (why), implying failure to obey orders; the officers' answer (v.46) reverses the authority structure entirely.
46: Οὐδέποτε οὕτως ἐλάλησεν ἄνθρωπος (Never did a man speak thus) — the officers sent to arrest Jesus return with a testimony instead. οὐδέποτε (G3763, never, at no time) is absolute; the officials sent by those who rejected him become unwilling witnesses. The word ἐλάλησεν (G2980, spoke) — not "did," not "performed signs," but simply "spoke": the content of his speech alone was sufficient to arrest their mission.
47: Μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς πεπλάνησθε; (Have you also been led astray?) — G4105 perfect passive of planaō (to deceive/lead astray): the state of having been deceived. The Pharisees frame the officers' astonishment as seduction rather than discernment. ὑμεῖς (you — emphatic) marks the scandal: even those they sent cannot be trusted. The question accuses; it does not inquire.
48: Μή τις ἐκ τῶν ἀρχόντων ἐπίστευσεν εἰς αὐτόν (Has any of the rulers believed in him?) — the rhetorical strategy is argument from authority: since no Sanhedrin member or Pharisee believes, the crowd's belief is discredited. The particle mē expects a negative answer. The Evangelist holds this up to dramatic irony: Nicodemus (v.50) is one of them and will speak, and Joseph of Arimathea, also a member, will act at the burial (19:38).
49: ὁ ὄχλος οὗτος ὁ μὴ γινώσκων τὸν νόμον ἐπάρατοί εἰσιν — ἐπάρατος (G1944, accursed) applied to the crowd who do not know the nomos. The Pharisees' logic: ignorance of Torah is itself a form of covenant exclusion. But the irony cuts back: they themselves have just misapplied Torah logic (v.23–24), and will attempt to judge without hearing (v.51). The claim that Torah-literacy protects from deception is being undercut by their own behavior in this very scene.
50: λέγει Νικόδημος πρὸς αὐτούς, ὁ ἐλθὼν πρὸς αὐτὸν πρότερον (Nicodemus says to them — he who came to him before) — the Evangelist identifies Nicodemus by his prior visit (John 3:1–15), now intervening as a voice of procedural fairness within the Sanhedrin. εἷς ὢν ἐξ αὐτῶν (being one of them) — this is the crux; he speaks from within the body, not as an outsider. His intervention is not yet a declaration of faith, but it is a public dissent.
51: Μὴ ὁ νόμος ἡμῶν κρίνει τὸν ἄνθρωπον (Does our law judge a man?) — Nicodemus invokes the nomos (G3551) they have just deployed against the crowd. The procedural principle: the law requires ἀκοῦσαι παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ (hearing from him first) and determining what he does before judgment. Deut 1:16–17 and 17:4 require full inquiry before verdict. This is legal argument from within their own framework, making it harder to dismiss.
52: Ἐρεύνησον καὶ ἴδε (G2045, search and see) — the imperative ereunēson (search the scriptures) is ironic: they tell Nicodemus to search the very scriptures he has implicitly defended as procedural authority. ὅτι προφήτης ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας οὐκ ἐγείρεται (that a prophet does not arise from Galilee) — their textual claim is actually weak; Jonah was from Gath-hepher in Galilee (2 Kgs 14:25). The Evangelist again records their scripture-citation as self-undermining.
53: The verse closes the Tabernacles episode: everyone went to their own home (εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ). A textual note: this verse begins the pericope adulterae (7:53–8:11), which is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts of John (P66, P75, Codex Vaticanus, Codex Sinaiticus) and appears in various locations across the manuscript tradition. The vast majority of textual critics regard it as a non-Johannine insertion, likely authentic tradition from another context. The MKT includes it but its linguistic style differs markedly from the surrounding chapters.
